sound_type: All pods reunion -- full SRKW greeting ceremony
era: Late summer / early autumn when Chinook salmon runs concentrate all three pods in the Salish Sea. The rarest marine acoustic event in Pacific Northwest waters. THE SPS SUMMIT COMPLETES.
ecosystem: Salish Sea (Puget Sound / Strait of Juan de Fuca / southern Strait of Georgia)
season: Late summer / early autumn (peak salmon runs driving pod convergence)
family: Orcinus orca -- Southern Resident Killer Whale community
population: ~73 individuals as of 2024: J Pod (~25) + K Pod (~17) + L Pod (~31)

theory_nodes.concept: The SRKW community structure: J Pod (~25 individuals, most studied, most frequently in Puget Sound), K Pod (~17 individuals, extended offshore range, follows Chinook south), L Pod (~31 individuals, largest pod, widest range from Monterey Bay to southeast Alaska). Total ~73 individuals = the entire surviving Southern Resident Killer Whale community. Genetically distinct from all other killer whale populations. Three pods, three dialects, one community. Critically endangered, ESA listed 2005. Primary threats: Chinook salmon decline (main prey), vessel noise masking echolocation and communication, accumulated contaminants (PCBs, PBDEs).
theory_nodes.description: The SRKW community -- J, K, L Pods, ~73 individuals, the entire community, critically endangered.

theory_nodes.concept: The greeting ceremony behavior: pods swim toward each other, pause at the surface 50-100 meters apart, then mingle with maximum physical and acoustic activity. Body rubbing, spy-hopping, lobtailing, breaching. Call rates increase dramatically during greeting (from ~1-5 per minute per individual normally to 10-20+ per minute). With ~73 individuals simultaneously at elevated rates: 700-1,500+ discrete call events per minute. Three pod-specific dialects (J Pod ~16 call types, K Pod ~12, L Pod ~14) interweaving simultaneously. Dialects remain acoustically identifiable even during high-density multi-pod events. The event is not noise -- it is a complex multi-layered structure of cultural significance.
theory_nodes.description: The greeting ceremony -- three dialects, 700-1,500+ call events per minute, the community's complete cultural vocabulary.

theory_nodes.concept: The acoustic profile: calls 500-20,000 Hz (communication), peak energy 1,000-8,000 Hz; echolocation clicks to 80 kHz+; surface behaviors (adult male breach 6 tons) generating additional acoustic events. During the full greeting the water column from 500 Hz to 80 kHz is occupied by SRKW sounds. The Orca Network and research organizations maintain hydrophone arrays in the San Juan Islands area with detailed recordings of greeting ceremonies. Primary research location: Haro Strait between San Juan Island and southern Vancouver Island (peak summer Chinook feeding ground, most likely convergence site).
theory_nodes.description: Full acoustic profile -- 500 Hz to 80 kHz+, three dialects, surface behaviors, hydrophone archive.

theory_nodes.concept: The Chinook salmon dependency: SRKW are Chinook specialists -- dietary preference so strong they will not eat other available prey even in apparent starvation. Population tracks Chinook abundance directly. Fraser River Chinook runs (July-September) draw all three pods to the San Juan Islands / Haro Strait area. The full tri-pod reunion is most likely during these summer salmon concentrations. The full greeting is therefore both an acoustic maximum and an indicator of salmon ecosystem health: when all three pods converge, the salmon are running.
theory_nodes.description: Chinook dependency -- the salmon that drives the reunion, the ecosystem health indicator.

theory_nodes.concept: E=10 calibration -- rarity + scale + irreplaceability + acoustic complexity: (1) Rarity: K and L Pods range widely offshore, full tri-pod greeting may occur only a handful of times per year. (2) Scale: ~73 individuals = the entire community. (3) Irreplaceability: three-dialect cultural archive transmitted across generations, unique on Earth. Pod loss = permanent acoustic archive loss. (4) Acoustic complexity: three dialects + surface behaviors + full-spectrum 500 Hz to 80 kHz vocalization. All four at maximum simultaneously = E=10.
theory_nodes.description: E=10 -- rarity + scale + irreplaceability + acoustic complexity. All four dimensions at maximum.

theory_nodes.concept: The terminal pairing with Nirvana Nevermind: both Pacific Northwest, both irreplaceable, both about populations that erupted into unexpected visibility. Nirvana from Aberdeen underground to global cultural dominance (Billboard 200 #1, Michael Jackson displaced). SRKW as the most photographed, most acoustically documented, most internationally recognized cetacean population in North America -- visibility that brought conservation attention and legal protection. Shared Salish Sea geography: the waters visible from Seattle are the home range of the terminal SPS entry.
theory_nodes.description: Terminal pairing with Nirvana -- both Pacific Northwest, both irreplaceable, both erupted into visibility.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. THE TERMINAL PAIRING. The summit's fourth and final E=20. Both Pacific Northwest. Both irreplaceable. Cultural detonation + endangered community reunion.

Both E=10. Consecutive SPS entries at the absolute ceiling. Terrestrial apex predator maximum (North Cascades wilderness) followed by marine apex community maximum (Salish Sea). Adjacent E=10 entries, complementary ecological domains.

Previous SPS E=10 (with Botch). Full SRKW greeting is the second SPS E=10 in the summit section. Both are multi-species maximum acoustic events; the SRKW greeting is species-specific community maximum rather than cross-species convergence.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. THE ENGINE'S TERMINAL PAIRING. The summit's fourth and final E=20. Both irreplaceable. Both Pacific Northwest. The cultural detonation and the endangered community's rarest reunion. The engine completes.

srkw_ecology: ~73 individuals total (2024). ESA critically endangered 2005. Primary prey: Chinook salmon. Primary threats: prey limitation (Chinook decline), vessel noise masking, PCB/PBDE contamination. Research: Center for Whale Research annual census, Orca Network hydrophone arrays San Juan Islands, The Whale Museum Friday Harbor. Haro Strait primary summer feeding/observation area.
